---
title: "Module 13: Fine-Tuning"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    highlight-style: monokai
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## Four Ways to Give AI Knowledge {.smaller}

Prompting and skills customize *how* an LLM responds. But what if you need it to know [private data]{.amber} it wasn't trained on?

| | **Long Context** | **RAG** | **Fine-Tuning** | **Small Language Model** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Short docs that fit in one prompt | Large or many docs; proprietary facts | Specific tone, format, or domain expertise | Full control, privacy, specialized tasks |
| **Data freshness** | Real-time (paste it in) | Real-time updates | Stale after training | Stale after training |
| **Cost to start** | None | Low (no model training) | Medium (GPU hours) | High (training + hosting) |
| **Cost per query** | High (long prompts are expensive) | Low (only relevant chunks sent) | Low | Low |
| **Complexity** | None | Moderate (pipeline + vector DB) | High (dataset curation) | Very high (full ML ops) |

## What is Fine-Tuning? {.section-divider}

## What is Fine-Tuning?

A pretrained LLM already knows language. **Fine-tuning** adjusts its weights on a smaller, task-specific dataset so it behaves differently:

- Adopt a specific tone or format (e.g., always respond as a financial analyst)
- Learn domain terminology and conventions
- Follow company-specific rules and workflows

:::{.explainer}
Analogy: a pretrained model is like hiring a smart generalist. Fine-tuning is [specialized on-the-job training]{.amber}.
:::

## Fine-Tuning vs. Prompting vs. RAG

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Prompting / Skills**]{.card-title}

- Change behavior via instructions
- No training required
- Limited by context window
- Easy to iterate
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**RAG**]{.card-title}

- Inject external knowledge at query time
- No model weights changed
- Answers grounded in retrieved documents
- Great for factual Q&A
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**Fine-Tuning**]{.card-title}

- Change the model's weights
- Learns patterns, tone, and format
- Knowledge baked into the model
- Best for consistent, specialized behavior
:::
:::

## When to Fine-Tune

Fine-tuning is worth the effort when:

- You need [consistent behavior]{.amber} across thousands of queries (not one-off tasks)
- Prompting alone can't capture the nuance (complex formatting, domain jargon, regulatory style)
- You want lower latency and cost than a large model with a long system prompt
- You have (or can generate) high-quality training examples

Fine-tuning is **not** the right tool when:

- Your data changes frequently (use RAG instead)
- You need answers from specific documents (use RAG)
- A well-crafted prompt already works (keep it simple)

## The Fine-Tuning Process {.section-divider}

## Step 1: Collect Training Data

Training data is a set of **input/output pairs** --- examples of what you want the model to do.

| **Input** | **Output** |
|---|---|
| "Summarize this 10-K risk factor..." | "The company faces supply chain risk due to..." |
| "Classify this earnings call sentence..." | "Positive guidance" |
| "Write a credit memo for..." | "Credit Assessment: BBB+ ..." |

:::{.explainer}
Start small. Google's Gemma docs note that behavioral changes can appear with as few as [20 examples]{.amber}. Build from there.
:::

## Step 2: Choose an Approach

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Full Fine-Tuning**]{.card-title}

- Update **all** model parameters
- Maximum flexibility
- Requires significant GPU memory and compute
- Produces a full copy of the model
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**LoRA (Parameter-Efficient)**]{.card-title}

- Freeze the original weights
- Train only small adapter matrices
- 10,000x fewer trainable parameters
- Same quality, fraction of the cost
:::
:::

:::{.explainer}
In practice, almost everyone uses [LoRA]{.amber} or a similar parameter-efficient method. Full fine-tuning is reserved for large-budget projects.
:::

## LoRA: The Key Idea {.section-divider}

## Why LoRA Works

The LoRA paper (Hu et al., 2021) made a key observation: when you fine-tune a large model, the weight changes have [low intrinsic rank]{.amber}.

The changes needed to adapt a model to a new task live in a much lower-dimensional space than the full parameter space.

This means you don't need to update billions of parameters --- you can capture the same adaptation with far fewer.

## LoRA: How It Works

Instead of learning a full weight update $\Delta W$ (dimensions $d \times d$), decompose it into two small matrices:

$$\Delta W = A \times B$$

where $A$ is $d \times r$ and $B$ is $r \times d$, and $r$ (the rank) is tiny: typically 4, 8, or 16.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Full Fine-Tuning**]{.card-title}

- Update $d^2$ parameters per layer
- For $d = 4096$: **16.8 million** parameters
- Full copy of model weights
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**LoRA (rank 16)**]{.card-title}

- Update $2 \times d \times r$ parameters per layer
- For $d = 4096$, $r = 16$: **131,072** parameters
- 128x reduction per layer
:::
:::

## LoRA at Inference Time

During fine-tuning, the original weights are [frozen]{.amber}:

$$y = W_{\text{original}}(x) + A \times B(x)$$

At deployment, merge the adapter into the base weights:

$$W_{\text{new}} = W_{\text{original}} + A \times B$$

- [No inference latency penalty]{.amber} --- the merged model runs at normal speed
- Adapter files are small (MBs, not GBs)
- Swap adapters on the same base model for different tasks

## QLoRA: Even More Efficient

**QLoRA** (Dettmers et al., 2023) adds quantization on top of LoRA:

1. Compress base model weights to [4-bit precision]{.amber} (from 16-bit) --- 4x memory savings
2. Train LoRA adapters on top of the quantized model
3. Paged optimizers handle memory spikes

:::{.explainer}
Result: fine-tune a **65B-parameter model on a single 48GB GPU**. Training is ~39% slower due to quantization overhead, but quality is comparable to full-precision LoRA.
:::

## Popular Fine-Tuning Datasets {.section-divider}

## Datasets for Fine-Tuning {.smaller}

| **Dataset** | **Size** | **Source** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stanford Alpaca** | 52K examples | GPT-3.5 generated | Instruction-following pairs; fine-tuned LLaMA 7B for < $600 |
| **Databricks Dolly** | 15K examples | Human-written | 7 task types: Q&A, summarization, classification, creative writing |
| **LIMA** | 1,000 examples | Curated by Meta | "Less Is More for Alignment" --- high quality beats high quantity |
| **OpenAssistant** | 161K messages | Crowdsourced | Multi-turn conversations in 35 languages with quality ratings |
| **UltraChat** | 1.5M dialogues | ChatGPT generated | Large-scale multi-round conversations; filtered subset of 200K commonly used |

:::{.explainer}
LIMA's finding is striking: [1,000 carefully curated examples]{.amber} can outperform 52,000 generated ones. Data quality matters more than quantity.
:::

## Distillation: LLM → Small Model {.section-divider}

## Why Not Just Use the Big Model?

A powerful pattern: use a large model to [generate training data]{.amber}, then fine-tune a smaller model on it.

Why bother? Why not just use the LLM directly?

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-dark}
[**Cost**]{.card-title}

Large models cost 10--30x more per query than small models

Generate 50K examples once ($200--500), then run the small model [forever]{.amber} at a fraction of the cost
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**Speed**]{.card-title}

A 7B model generates responses in 50--200ms on modest hardware

Large API models take 1--5 seconds

Critical for real-time applications
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**Privacy**]{.card-title}

A fine-tuned small model runs [entirely on your infrastructure]{.amber}

No data leaves your network

Essential for healthcare, legal, and financial applications (HIPAA, GDPR)
:::
:::

## Distillation in Practice

Well-known examples of LLM → small model distillation:

- **Stanford Alpaca**: GPT-3.5 → LLaMA 7B. Cost: $600. Comparable performance on many tasks.
- **Microsoft Orca**: GPT-4's detailed [reasoning traces]{.amber} (not just answers) → smaller model. Capturing the teacher's thinking process improves quality.
- **Microsoft Phi**: LLM-generated "textbook quality" synthetic data → 1.3B--3B parameter models that punch far above their weight.

:::{.explainer}
The pattern works best for [specific, repeatable tasks at scale]{.amber}: classify emails, extract fields, generate reports. For diverse, one-off tasks, just use the LLM directly.
:::

## Frameworks and Tools {.section-divider}

## Fine-Tuning Frameworks

Google's Gemma documentation lists several popular tools:

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Beginner-Friendly**]{.card-title}

- **Hugging Face + PEFT** --- most popular; LoRA built in
- **Unsloth** --- 2x faster training, lower memory
- **Keras** --- familiar API, LoRA support
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Production / Scale**]{.card-title}

- **Axolotl** --- config-driven, many model architectures
- **LLaMA Factory** --- GUI for fine-tuning
- **Google Cloud Vertex AI** --- managed service, multi-GPU
:::
:::

:::{.explainer}
For most use cases, start with [Hugging Face + PEFT]{.amber} or [Unsloth]{.amber}. Both support LoRA/QLoRA out of the box and have extensive documentation.
:::

## Step 3: Test Your Model

Google recommends three categories of test cases:

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Success Tests**]{.card-title}

Inputs the model should always handle correctly

- Core use case queries
- Common variations
- Expected formats
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Failure Tests**]{.card-title}

Inputs the model should [refuse]{.amber} or flag

- Out-of-scope requests
- Adversarial prompts
- Requests for confidential data
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Boundary Tests**]{.card-title}

Edge cases that test limits

- Ambiguous inputs
- Missing context
- Mixed-language queries
:::
:::

## Step 4: Deploy

For LoRA-tuned models, deployment is straightforward:

- Deploy the **base model** + the **LoRA adapter** (a small file)
- Or **merge** the adapter into the base weights for a single model file
- Serve with standard inference tools (vLLM, TGI, Ollama)

:::{.explainer}
Multiple LoRA adapters can share a single base model. A compliance adapter, a customer-service adapter, and a research adapter can all run on the [same GPU]{.amber}, swapping adapters per request.
:::
